turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court in case 16-9493, Rosales-Mireles v. United States.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Federal Rule of Criminal Procedure 52(b) and this Court's decision in United States v. Olano, provide that a Court of Appeals may consider errors that are plain or obvious and affected defendant's substantial rights even though they are raised for the first time on appeal. It is well-established under what is often called Olano's fourth prong that courts should correct such an error if it seriously affects the fairness, integrity or public reputation of judicial proceedings. This case concerns when a miscalculation of the United States Sentencing Guideline range satisfies Olano's fourth prong. During federal sentencing proceedings the US Probation Office typically prepares a report that calculates the guideline range that applies to a defendant's case. In this case the Probation Office made a mistake in Petitioner Rosales-Mireles' report and recommended a guidelines range that was higher than it should have been. Neither the District Court nor the parties noted the error, however, so the District Court relied on the miscalculated guideline range when it determined Rosales-Mireles' sentence. On appeal Rosales-Mireles challenged the incorrect guidelines range for the first time. The Fifth Circuit concluded that the guidelines' error was plain and that it affected Rosales-Mireles' substantial rights because there was a reasonable probability that he would have been subject to a different sentence but for the error. The Fifth Circuit declined to remand the case for resentencing however, concluding that Rosales-Mireles had not established that the error met Olano's fourth prong because neither the error nor the resulting sentence would shock the conscience. Rosales-Mireles petitions for certiorari and we now reverse. The Fifth Circuit applied an inappropriately high standard for determining when an error seriously affects the fairness, integrity or judicial reputation of proceedings. It shocked the conscience standard is not reflected in Rule 52(b) nor in how the plain error doctrine has been applied by this Court. In fact, a plain guidelines error that affects a defendant's substantial rights is precisely the type of error that ordinarily warrants relief under 52(b). The fairness, integrity and the public reputation of judicial proceedings would be undermined if courts refused to correct errors that are likely to result in longer prison sentences than necessary. That derived from judicial error that are relatively inexpensive to correct and that hamper the goal of achieving uniformity and proportionality in sentencing. The United States in the dissent agree that the Fifth Circuit standard was inaccurate but they maintained that Rosales-Mireles is still not entitled to relief. In their view the sentence he received is still reasonable and does not constitute an exceptional circumstance warranting relief under Rule 52(b). But the circumstances of Rosales-Mireles' case are exceptional under this Court's precedent, and there are no countervailing factors that otherwise further the fairness, integrity or public reputation of the proceedings. Moreover regardless of its ultimate reasonableness a sentence that lacks reliability because of unjust procedures may well undermine the public perception of the proceedings. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Thomas has filed the dissenting opinion in which Justice Alito has joined.
